name: Kitchen Remodels
h1: Kitchen Remodels in Santa Cruz, California
metaTitle: Santa Cruz Kitchen Remodel Contractor | Gravity Construction
metaDescription: Licensed Santa Cruz kitchen remodel contractor. Custom cabinetry, layout changes, counters, appliances and lighting — built to last in coastal homes. Free estimates. CSLB #1075110.
lede: From a straightforward cabinet-and-counter refresh to a wall-removing, layout-changing transformation — we handle Santa Cruz kitchen remodels end-to-end, with the owner on site from demo through punch list.
bodyHtml:
Why Santa Cruz kitchens are different

Santa Cruz homes span redwood cabins in Ben Lomond, Craftsman bungalows on the Westside, mid-century tract houses in Live Oak, and ocean-adjacent builds in Capitola and Aptos. Every one of those has kitchen quirks a templated remodeler misses — settling foundations in the hills, salt-air corrosion on hardware near the coast, undersized 1950s panels that can't handle an induction range, and original knob-and-tube that has to come out before drywall goes back on.

We plan for that up front so you don't get surprise change orders three weeks into demo.

What a Gravity kitchen remodel includes

  • Design consult — we walk the space with you, talk through how you actually cook and entertain, and sketch layout options (galley vs. L vs. island) with realistic cost impact for each.
  • Permits — pulled through City of Santa Cruz, County of Santa Cruz, or Capitola/Scotts Valley as appropriate. You never interact with the permit office; we do.
  • Demo and disposal — we protect flooring, seal off the rest of the house, and haul everything out clean.
  • Structural changes — wall removal with proper beam sizing, headers engineered and stamped when required.
  • Electrical — new circuits for range, dishwasher, disposal, microwave; GFCI/AFCI to current code; under-cabinet lighting; can lights; outlet placement that actually works for how you use the counter.
  • Plumbing — sink relocation, supply and drain updates, pot filler roughs, filtered or instant-hot water lines.
  • HVAC — range hood venting to exterior (not recirculated), any duct relocation.
  • Cabinetry — semi-custom or fully custom, installed tight and shimmed square. Soft-close everywhere.
  • Countertops — quartz, quartzite, granite, butcher block, soapstone — templated after cabinets are in, not before.
  • Tile + backsplash — proper substrate, waterproofing behind wet walls, grout color matched to stone.
  • Appliance install — ranges, refrigerators, wall ovens, vent hoods, wine fridges — all integrated cleanly.
  • Finish carpentry — crown, toe kicks, panel-ready fridge fronts, open shelving, custom pantry interiors.
  • Final walkthrough + warranty — we fix punch-list items on our dime, and we stand behind the work.

What does a kitchen remodel cost in Santa Cruz?

Ranges vary with scope, but as of 2026 a realistic ballpark in Santa Cruz County is:

  • Cosmetic refresh (paint, hardware, counters, backsplash, maybe appliances; same layout): $35,000–$65,000
  • Mid-range remodel (new cabinets and counters, some electrical/plumbing updates, same footprint): $65,000–$120,000
  • Full remodel with layout changes (wall removal, moved plumbing, island added, full appliance suite): $120,000–$250,000+

You'll get a detailed written estimate that breaks those numbers down line by line — no vague allowances that balloon mid-project.

Timeline

A typical Santa Cruz kitchen remodel runs 6–12 weeks from demo day to final walkthrough. Custom cabinetry lead times (8–16 weeks) usually set the pace; we start fabrication well before demo so cabinets arrive when the boxes are ready.

Getting started

Call (831) 234-4669 or email dhollinga6@gmail.com. Send a few photos of your current kitchen and a note about what's working and what isn't — we'll come out, measure, and put real numbers on paper.

faq.q: Can you work around me if I live in the house during the remodel?
faq.a: Yes — we set up a temporary kitchen zone, seal off the work area with dust walls, and time water/electric shutoffs so you're never blindsided. Most clients stay in the home for a kitchen remodel.

faq.q: Do I need a permit for my Santa Cruz kitchen remodel?
faq.a: If you're changing plumbing locations, moving gas, adding electrical circuits, or removing walls — yes. We pull everything through the appropriate City or County permit office as part of the project.

faq.q: Do you handle the design, or do I need a designer first?
faq.a: Both options work. We do space-planning and materials selection in-house for most remodels. For whole-home-scale projects we partner with Santa Cruz-area designers and architects.

name: Bath Remodels
h1: Bathroom Remodels in Santa Cruz, California
metaTitle: Santa Cruz Bathroom Remodel Contractor | Gravity Construction
metaDescription: Santa Cruz bathroom remodel contractor — tile, tubs, custom showers, vanities, plumbing upgrades. Waterproofing done right for coastal climates. CSLB #1075110. Free estimates.
lede: Bathrooms are where sloppy work comes back to haunt you. We build Santa Cruz bathroom remodels to last — with real waterproofing, proper slope, and plumbing that'll pass inspection the first time.
bodyHtml:
Built to survive coastal moisture

Near the Santa Cruz coast, salt air and high humidity eat cheap fixtures in two years. We spec corrosion-resistant hardware, use closed-cell foam or mineral wool in wet-wall cavities, and waterproof with Schluter Kerdi or RedGard systems behind every tile shower — not just the old-school tar paper that gets you through the final but fails at year three.

What's included

  • Full demo to studs where needed — we find and fix any existing water damage before new tile goes up
  • Plumbing rough-in: valve replacement, new shower rough, sink and toilet relocation if layout is changing
  • Electrical: GFCI circuits, in-floor heat wiring, fan and light on separate switches, dedicated vanity outlets
  • Waterproofing: membrane-based systems (Schluter/RedGard) with proper lapping onto the pan and over curbs
  • Shower pans: poured mud, linear drains, curbless walk-in builds when the subfloor allows
  • Tile: large-format porcelain, subway, Zellige, mosaics — set flat with proper lippage and grout joint sizing
  • Vanity and counters: custom or semi-custom with stone or quartz tops
  • Heated floors: Nuheat or Warmup mats under tile — a small upcharge that pays back fast
  • Ventilation: properly sized exhaust fan vented to exterior, not into the attic (which violates code and grows mold)

Popular Santa Cruz bath remodel types

  • Hall bath refresh ($15,000–$35,000) — tub-to-shower conversion, new vanity, tile, fixtures
  • Primary bath remodel ($40,000–$90,000) — custom walk-in shower, freestanding tub, double vanity, heated floors
  • Aging-in-place conversion ($20,000–$55,000) — curbless shower, grab-bar backing, comfort-height toilet, lever handles
  • Full primary suite build-out ($75,000–$150,000+) — layout changes, reconfigured closet, window additions

Timeline

A straightforward bath remodel is 3–6 weeks. Primary baths with custom shower builds, heated floors, and vanity fabrication run 6–10 weeks.

Why waterproofing matters more than anything else

We've done plenty of remodels where we've torn out previous "remodels" from other contractors and found mold behind the tile, rotted subfloor under the shower pan, and fans vented into the attic instead of outside. Those are the kinds of shortcuts that save the other guy a day and cost you $15,000 to fix four years later. We don't take them.

faq.q: Can you do a tub-to-shower conversion?
faq.a: Yes — very common request. We remove the tub, rebuild the curb/pan, install new waterproofing, and finish in tile or solid surface. A standard hall-bath conversion takes about 3 weeks.

faq.q: What's the difference between a $15k and a $50k bath remodel?
faq.a: Scope and materials. A $15k job is typically a cosmetic update in an existing footprint with stock fixtures. A $50k job often includes layout changes, custom tile work, heated floors, and higher-end fixtures and stone. We'll help you match scope to budget honestly.

faq.q: Do you handle the plumbing permit?
faq.a: Yes — every permit is pulled under our license. You never deal with the County or City directly.

name: ADUs & Home Additions
h1: ADU Builders & Home Additions in Santa Cruz County
metaTitle: Santa Cruz ADU Builder | Accessory Dwelling Units & Additions
metaDescription: Santa Cruz ADU builder — detached, attached, and garage-conversion ADUs across Santa Cruz County. SB 9 / state ADU law, permits, design-build. CSLB #1075110.
lede: California's ADU laws make adding a second unit or expanding your home easier than it's ever been — but the local permit process still takes experience. We've navigated it across Santa Cruz, Capitola, Scotts Valley, and the unincorporated County.
bodyHtml:
What California's ADU laws unlocked

Under state law (and Santa Cruz County's ADU ordinance) most single-family lots can add at least one ADU up to 800–1,200 sq ft plus a junior ADU. Setbacks are relaxed, parking requirements are often waived, and the ministerial approval process means cities can't arbitrarily block well-designed projects. The upside: you can add rental income, a separate guest or in-law space, or future caregiver housing — all while materially boosting your property value.

The catch: every Santa Cruz County jurisdiction interprets the rules slightly differently, and the fire-department clearance, utility tie-in, and septic/sewer logistics are where projects bog down. That's the part experience saves you on.

Types of ADUs we build

  • Detached new-build ADU — ground-up structure on the same lot as the main house. Typically 400–1,200 sq ft. Best for lots with enough yard and utility access.
  • Attached ADU — addition with its own entrance that shares a wall with the main home. Often cheaper per sq ft because you're sharing foundation and some utilities.
  • Garage-conversion ADU — turning an existing attached or detached garage into a full living unit. Fastest path and often the lowest-cost, as long as the structure is sound.
  • Junior ADU (JADU) — up to 500 sq ft carved out of the main house, sharing a bathroom or kitchen access in some cases. Great for in-law suites.
  • Home additions — adding square footage to the main house (primary suite, larger kitchen, office) rather than creating a separate unit.

What's involved

A typical ADU timeline from first meeting to move-in day:

  1. Feasibility review (1–2 weeks) — we check your lot against the current ADU ordinance, flag setback/utility/slope issues early
  2. Design (4–10 weeks) — working with you and a local architect or designer on plans that maximize the unit's value without overbuilding
  3. Permit submittal (3–6 months for approval, depending on jurisdiction and project complexity)
  4. Site prep + foundation (2–4 weeks)
  5. Framing, roofing, rough-ins (6–10 weeks)
  6. Finishes + final inspections (6–10 weeks)

Start-to-move-in: 10–16 months for a detached ADU; 6–10 months for a garage conversion.

ADU cost in Santa Cruz County

As of 2026, budget ranges we see on real projects:

  • Garage-conversion ADU: $180,000–$300,000
  • Detached 400–600 sq ft ADU: $350,000–$500,000
  • Detached 800–1,200 sq ft ADU: $500,000–$800,000
  • Primary-suite addition: $180,000–$400,000

Ranges depend heavily on finishes, site conditions (hillside lots and septic systems cost more), and whether you're keeping existing utilities or bringing in new service.

Permit-savvy + local

We work with the Santa Cruz County Planning Department, City of Santa Cruz Planning, Capitola Community Development, and Scotts Valley regularly. We know which reviewers are sticklers for energy-calcs, which jurisdictions flag hillside grading fast, and how to position a set of plans for quick approval.

faq.q: Can I rent out my ADU on Airbnb?
faq.a: Short-term rental rules vary by jurisdiction. City of Santa Cruz, for example, restricts non-hosted short-term rentals. County of Santa Cruz has its own rules. We'll flag the limitations for your lot during the feasibility review.

faq.q: Do I need to add parking for the ADU?
faq.a: Under current state law, no new parking is required for ADUs on lots within a half-mile of transit or in historic districts. Many Santa Cruz lots qualify. We verify during feasibility.

faq.q: How does an ADU affect my property taxes?
faq.a: California's Prop 19 means only the new ADU square footage is reassessed — your existing home's tax basis stays intact. Check with your accountant for specifics.

faq.q: Can I finance an ADU?
faq.a: Yes — construction loans, HELOCs, and CalHFA's ADU grant programs are all common paths. We can recommend local lenders who do a lot of ADU work.

name: New Home Construction
h1: Custom Home Builder in Santa Cruz, California
metaTitle: Santa Cruz Custom Home Builder | New Construction | Gravity Construction
metaDescription: Santa Cruz custom home builder — ground-up new construction from coastal lots to redwood mountain properties. Owner-supervised, CSLB #1075110, fully insured.
lede: Building new in Santa Cruz County means navigating coastal commission review, steep-slope regulations, fire zone setbacks, and some of the toughest energy-code requirements in California. We've done it — and we build homes that'll still look right in 30 years.
bodyHtml:
What new construction in Santa Cruz actually involves

Ground-up construction is the hardest thing a builder does. On a Santa Cruz County lot you're likely dealing with some combination of: hillside grading and drainage, coastal commission or riparian setbacks, Title 24 all-electric code (as of 2023), seismic bracing for slope, stormwater management, oak-tree protection zones, and fire-hardening requirements in WUI (wildland-urban interface) zones. Plus the baseline — foundation, framing, MEP, finishes.

We work through it all with you: site evaluation, civil engineer referrals, structural engineering, Title 24 energy compliance, permit shepherding, and then the actual build with the owner on site every phase.

Our process

  1. Site walk + feasibility — we look at your lot before you design anything. Constraints drive design; getting them wrong costs you six figures.
  2. Team assembly — architect, civil engineer, structural engineer, soils engineer if needed. We have local Santa Cruz-area professionals we work with regularly.
  3. Design development — floor plans, exteriors, initial material choices. You iterate until it's right.
  4. Pre-construction services — detailed cost estimate, schedule, permit strategy. This is where we price real, not guess.
  5. Permit submittal and approval — typically 6–12 months in Santa Cruz County; coastal zone or hillside adds time.
  6. Construction — site prep, foundation, framing, rough-ins, shell complete, finishes, final inspections, certificate of occupancy. Usually 10–16 months.

What we build well

  • Modern coastal homes with durable detailing for salt air
  • Redwood mountain homes with defensible space and fire-hardening
  • Contemporary hillside builds with steel-and-concrete structural systems
  • Small-footprint, well-built custom homes for infill lots

Budget ranges (2026)

Every lot is different, but as a sanity-check:

  • Entry custom (simple lot, standard finishes): $600–$800 per sq ft
  • Mid-range custom (most projects): $800–$1,100 per sq ft
  • High-end custom (architectural, complex sites, premium finishes): $1,100–$1,600+ per sq ft

Site-prep costs — grading, drainage, utilities, driveway — can add $75,000 to $500,000 on top of the house itself, depending on lot difficulty.

Timeline reality

From the day you close on a lot to the day you move in, Santa Cruz County new construction is usually 18–30 months. Shorter for flat, serviced lots with straightforward designs. Longer for coastal-zone, hillside, or permit-complicated projects. We'll build a realistic schedule from day one — not a fantasy one.

faq.q: Do I need an architect?
faq.a: For a ground-up custom home in Santa Cruz County, essentially yes — you need stamped plans. We partner with local architects or can work from plans you bring to us.

faq.q: Are you all-electric, per California's 2023 code?
faq.a: Yes. New construction in California is now all-electric by default — heat pumps for HVAC and water heating, induction ranges. We've been building this way since the code switched.

faq.q: Can you do coastal commission work?
faq.a: Yes, we've built projects that required Coastal Development Permits. It's a process, but a predictable one when you know the path.

name: Whole-Home Remodels
h1: Whole-Home Remodels in Santa Cruz, California
metaTitle: Santa Cruz Whole Home Remodel Contractor | Gravity Construction
metaDescription: Whole-home remodel contractor in Santa Cruz County. Gut-to-studs renovations that modernize systems, layout, and finishes while respecting your home's character.
lede: A whole-home remodel is the chance to fix everything at once — the systems you hate, the layout that never worked, the finishes you grew out of. We handle them end-to-end: one contract, one supervisor, one clear path.
bodyHtml:
When a whole-home remodel is the right call

If three or more of these apply to your house, you're probably better off with a whole-home remodel than a series of disconnected projects:

  • Kitchen and at least one bath both need work
  • Electrical panel is undersized or the wiring is knob-and-tube, aluminum, or old cloth-sheathed
  • Plumbing is galvanized or polybutylene
  • Layout doesn't match how you live — walls in the wrong places
  • Windows are single-pane or failing
  • No central HVAC, or the existing system is 20+ years old
  • You're planning to stay in the house 10+ years

Tackling these as one project saves you duplicate permit fees, duplicate mobilization costs, and the hassle of living through half a dozen separate construction cycles.

What's involved

  • Gut demo or targeted demo depending on scope — we protect floors and fixtures we're keeping
  • System updates: new panel and circuits, PEX or copper replumb, high-efficiency heat pump HVAC, Title-24 compliant insulation and air sealing
  • Structural: wall removal, seismic retrofits where foundations lack proper connection (common in pre-1970s Santa Cruz homes), subfloor repair
  • Layout changes: opening up kitchen-to-living, moving primary bedroom for privacy, adding walk-in closets, improving indoor-outdoor flow
  • Windows and doors: dual-pane low-E throughout, proper flashing and water management
  • Exterior: siding repair or replacement, deck rebuild, landscaping prep
  • All new finishes: floors, trim, doors, paint, tile, fixtures, appliances, cabinetry

How we keep whole-home remodels sane

These are big projects. Two things separate a good one from a nightmare:

  1. A detailed, realistic estimate up front. We spec materials down to the fixture and tile before we give you a number — no "allowances" that balloon 40%.
  2. A single owner-supervisor on site. Damien walks the job every day. When something comes up — and on a whole-home remodel, it always does — a decision gets made fast and the job keeps moving.

Budget and timeline

Ranges for a typical Santa Cruz County whole-home remodel:

  • 1,500 sq ft mid-range: $400,000–$700,000, 6–10 months
  • 2,500 sq ft mid-to-high: $700,000–$1,500,000, 10–14 months
  • 3,500+ sq ft, high-end + structural: $1.5M–$3M+, 14–20 months

Most clients move out for the bulk of the work — it's faster for us, and less miserable for you.

faq.q: Is a whole-home remodel cheaper than tearing down and rebuilding?
faq.a: Usually yes, if the existing foundation and framing are sound. New construction hits $800–$1,400/sq ft; a whole-home remodel is typically $300–$700/sq ft. We'll honestly tell you when a teardown would serve you better.

faq.q: Do we have to move out?
faq.a: For whole-home scope, yes — typically for 6–14 months. We help you plan the transition. A few clients stay with extended family, others rent nearby.

faq.q: Can we keep the exterior the same and only remodel the inside?
faq.a: Absolutely. Many of our whole-home remodels leave the exterior envelope and just touch up siding and windows while gutting the interior.

name: Custom Finish Carpentry
h1: Custom Finish Carpentry in Santa Cruz
metaTitle: Custom Finish Carpentry Santa Cruz | Built-Ins, Trim, Millwork
metaDescription: Custom built-ins, trim, shelving, paneling, and millwork — crafted on site in Santa Cruz, California. Licensed general contractor. CSLB #1075110.
lede: The built-in bookcase that fits like it was always there. The wainscoting that matches the 1920s profile you inherited. The floor-to-ceiling pantry that hides the mess. Custom finish carpentry is where a house starts to feel custom.
bodyHtml:
What we build

  • Built-in bookcases and entertainment walls — sized to the room, trimmed to existing architecture
  • Window seats and reading nooks — with hinged lids for storage or drawers underneath
  • Wainscoting, beadboard, board-and-batten — matched to historic profiles on older homes, clean modern lines on contemporary ones
  • Custom pantries — pull-outs, drawer banks, sliding shelves, lazy susans
  • Mudrooms and drop zones — benches, cubbies, hooks, shoe storage
  • Closet systems — custom reach-ins and walk-ins with drawers, shelving, shoe racks, and hanging sections sized to your actual wardrobe
  • Crown molding, baseboards, door and window casings — matched to existing trim or designed new
  • Interior doors and hardware — solid-core replacements, pocket doors, barn doors on hardware
  • Stair rebuilds and custom railings — wood, iron, cable, or glass
  • Fireplace surrounds and mantels
  • Wet bars and coffee stations

Why on-site, not shop-built?

For built-ins, on-site fabrication almost always wins. Rooms are never perfectly square, floors are never perfectly flat, and corners are never exactly 90°. A built-in fabricated in a shop has to deal with all of that on install day — often with visible gaps or awkward scribes. On-site, we dry-fit as we go, scribe the face to the wall, and hang the finish so the piece reads as architecture, not furniture pushed against the wall.

For straightforward cabinet-box work (pantries, closets, some entertainment walls), shop-built boxes with on-site face frames and finish carpentry is the right blend of speed and fit. We pick the right approach per project.

Typical budgets

  • Small built-in or window seat: $3,500–$8,000
  • Full wall of built-ins with shelves, cabinets, and lighting: $12,000–$28,000
  • Whole-house trim package (base, case, crown on a 2,000 sq ft home): $18,000–$40,000
  • Stair rebuild with custom railing: $15,000–$45,000
  • Kitchen pantry build-out: $5,000–$15,000

faq.q: Do you do built-ins as a standalone project, or only as part of a bigger remodel?
faq.a: Both. We take standalone carpentry projects regularly — a wall of built-ins, a stair rebuild, a trim package.

faq.q: Can you match existing trim profiles in an older home?
faq.a: Yes. We take samples, source matching or custom-milled stock, and blend new work into original trim so you can't tell where old ends and new begins.

name: Commercial & Tenant Improvements
h1: Commercial Construction & Tenant Improvements in Santa Cruz
metaTitle: Santa Cruz Commercial Contractor | Tenant Improvements | Gravity Construction
metaDescription: Santa Cruz commercial general contractor — retail build-outs, office renovations, medical and dental TIs, ADA-compliant upgrades. Licensed CSLB #1075110. Free estimates.
lede: Commercial construction punishes sloppy schedules. Every day your space is offline is revenue you're not earning. We build commercial and tenant-improvement projects in Santa Cruz County with clear milestones, nightly cleanup, and the permit fluency to keep inspectors moving.
bodyHtml:
What we build on the commercial side

  • Retail build-outs — storefronts, showrooms, boutiques, specialty retail. Storefront glazing, accessible entries, POS and security rough-ins, display lighting.
  • Office tenant improvements — open-plan conversions, private-office buildouts, conference-room glass walls, new data and power drops, break rooms.
  • Medical and dental TIs — op rooms, sterilization areas, dedicated plumbing for chairs, lead-lined X-ray rooms, HIPAA-considerate layouts.
  • Professional services — law offices, accountants, therapists, salons. Sound-attenuated walls, private entries, ADA-compliant restrooms.
  • Mixed-use ground-floor commercial — shell-to-suite buildouts in new mixed-use buildings across downtown Santa Cruz and Capitola Village.
  • Façade and entry upgrades — storefront replacement, accessible entries, exterior signage support.

What separates commercial from residential

The building-code side changes fast. Commercial projects mean: California Building Code Chapter 11B accessibility compliance (path of travel, restroom clearances, counter heights, signage), Title 24 Part 6 commercial energy code, fire-and-life-safety review (occupant load, exiting, sprinklers, fire alarm), and often health-department review depending on use. We know which reviewers at Santa Cruz Building Division and Capitola Community Development flag what — and we design the submittal to pass first round, not third.

Working around an operating business

Most of our commercial work is in occupied buildings — adjacent tenants still need to open their doors. We plan for:

  • After-hours and weekend work for the loudest phases (demo, concrete cutting)
  • Sealed dust walls with negative-air HEPA filtration in healthcare and food spaces
  • Nightly cleanup so hallways and shared spaces read clean by morning
  • Loading-dock and freight-elevator scheduling that respects your landlord's rules
  • Clear communication with the property manager — we attend their weekly walkthrough if they have one

Budget ranges (2026)

  • Light office TI (paint, carpet, minor wall changes): $40–$80/sq ft
  • Standard office or retail buildout: $120–$250/sq ft
  • Medical or dental TI: $250–$500/sq ft
  • Full shell-to-suite with MEP: $200–$400/sq ft

Permit and schedule

Simple TIs that fit over-the-counter review can start construction 4–8 weeks from signed contract. Larger buildouts with full MEP and life-safety review run 10–20 weeks from contract to first day of demo. We give you a realistic calendar with the estimate — and we hit the dates we quote.

Who we work with

Commercial landlords, property managers, tenants negotiating new leases, and owner-users buying their own buildings. We're comfortable with landlord work letters, tenant allowances, and three-party coordination with architects and leasing brokers.

faq.q: Can you work with our architect, or do you bring your own?
faq.a: Both. Most commercial TIs come with a tenant's or landlord's architect already engaged — we work seamlessly with them. For smaller projects we can bring in a local commercial architect we've partnered with.

faq.q: Do you handle ADA path-of-travel upgrades?
faq.a: Yes — that's one of the most common scope items triggered by any commercial TI in California. We build it into the estimate up front so it isn't a surprise during plan check.

faq.q: Can you do night or weekend work to keep the business open?
faq.a: Yes. We've done phased projects where the business operated through construction, and after-hours jobs where all noisy work happened between 8pm and 6am. It costs a premium but it's often worth it.

faq.q: Are you bondable?
faq.a: Yes. Larger commercial projects that require performance or payment bonds — we can provide them through our surety.

name: Restaurant Build-Outs
h1: Restaurant, Café & Bar Build-Outs in Santa Cruz
metaTitle: Santa Cruz Restaurant Contractor | Kitchen Build-Outs & TIs | Gravity Construction
metaDescription: Santa Cruz restaurant and café contractor — Type I hoods, grease interceptors, health-department-compliant kitchens, full front-of-house build-outs. CSLB #1075110.
lede: Restaurant construction is its own discipline. Type I hoods, grease interceptors, Ansul systems, three-compartment sinks, hand sinks every eight feet, FRP walls, coved base, quarry tile — get any one of those wrong and the health inspector walks out without signing. We build restaurants in Santa Cruz County that open on schedule.
bodyHtml:
Restaurant build-outs we handle

  • New restaurants — full shell-to-suite: kitchen, dining room, bar, restrooms, storage, back-of-house
  • Café and coffee shops — espresso stations, pastry cases, small prep kitchens, front counters, outdoor seating
  • Bars and tasting rooms — bar-top fabrication, draft systems, glass-washer plumbing, ABC-compliant layouts
  • Quick-service and counter-service — line kitchens, POS stations, pickup windows, drive-throughs (where zoning allows)
  • Restaurant remodels — refreshing a space between tenants, expanding kitchen capacity, adding a patio, converting to full liquor
  • Kitchen-only upgrades — new hood and Ansul, larger line, walk-in replacement, grease-interceptor relocation

What makes a restaurant build-out different

You're coordinating Santa Cruz County Environmental Health, the fire marshal, the building department, and sometimes the ABC — plus your equipment vendors, POS installer, and a chef who needs the layout to actually work during service. We run that coordination:

  • Type I commercial hood — sized to equipment, UL-listed, ducted to exterior with welded seams, with make-up air balanced so the room isn't pulling negative when the line is firing
  • Ansul (or equivalent) fire suppression — designed, installed, and certified; tied into the shunt-trip and fire alarm
  • Grease interceptor — sized per the County's flow-rate formula, installed with access, plumbed so every fixture that can produce grease actually drains through it
  • Three-compartment sink + dedicated hand sinks + mop sink — all with the right air gaps, indirect waste where required, and backflow preventers
  • FRP or stainless wall panels + coved base + quarry tile or sealed concrete floor — every transition sealed; no harborage for contamination
  • Walk-in cooler and freezer — slab detail (usually depressed to get a flush entry), refrigeration lines, condensate routing, condenser placement
  • Dedicated HVAC — balanced with hood make-up air, properly filtered supply, separate return paths for kitchen vs. dining
  • Grease-duct fire rating — two-hour shaft wrap where the duct passes through occupied space, inspection access doors at required intervals

Front of house

Beyond the kitchen, the dining experience is what brings guests back. We do:

  • Custom millwork — bar tops, hostess stands, banquettes, shelving
  • Sound treatment — acoustic ceiling clouds, fabric panels, soft seating that keeps a full room conversational
  • Lighting design — task lighting at every station, warm ambient over dining, dimmable zones
  • Restrooms — fully ADA-compliant with health-department-acceptable finishes
  • Outdoor patios and parklets where zoning allows — permitted, lit, and heated for year-round use

Timelines and budgets

  • Small café / coffee shop buildout (1,000–1,500 sq ft): $400,000–$800,000 / 12–18 weeks
  • Full-service restaurant (2,500–4,000 sq ft): $900,000–$2.2M / 16–28 weeks
  • Bar / tasting room: $350,000–$900,000 / 10–16 weeks
  • Kitchen-only refresh with new hood and equipment: $150,000–$450,000 / 6–10 weeks

Equipment lead times drive schedule more than construction does. Hoods, walk-ins, and cooking lines can run 8–14 weeks. We order early so the gear lands when the rough-ins are ready.

Working with Environmental Health

Santa Cruz County Environmental Health plan-checks every restaurant and has an inspector walk the space before opening. We know their requirements line by line and we build to them the first time — no reopening walls after inspection. We've never had a restaurant fail its final Environmental Health inspection.

faq.q: We signed a lease. How fast can we open?
faq.a: For a standard buildout in a space that already had restaurant use: 4–6 months from signed contract to doors open. For a conversion from retail or office to a new restaurant, plan on 6–9 months because of the Environmental Health plan check and utility upgrades.

faq.q: Do you handle the equipment package?
faq.a: We coordinate closely with the equipment vendor you choose (or recommend one). We handle rough-ins, final connections, hood and Ansul install, and certification. You own the equipment contract so warranties stay clean.

faq.q: Can you do a ghost-kitchen or commissary buildout?
faq.a: Yes — we've built production kitchens for catering and delivery-only concepts. Same code path as a restaurant kitchen, just without the front of house.

faq.q: What about ABC (liquor license) requirements?
faq.a: We build to what ABC needs — proper dispensing areas, minor-entry restrictions for Type 48 bars, required signage locations. The license application itself is your attorney's or consultant's job, but we make sure the space passes the ABC walkthrough.
